task-d6-G-C8O.name: Inventory all removed parts and new parts waiting to install
task-d6-G-C8O.agentRationale: 8 years of stalled project means parts are scattered. We need a full list before planning anything — avoids buying duplicates and reveals what's missing.

parts.name: Spark plugs x6 — Champion RN12YC or NGK equivalent for AMC 258

task-sQmcUCW3.name: Check engine condition — look for obvious damage, missing parts, and try turning crank by hand
task-sQmcUCW3.agentRationale: Engine has been sitting for 8 years. Need to know if it's seized, missing components, or leaking before planning any drivetrain work.
steps: Remove the air filter housing to get a clear view of the top of the engine — look for cracked intake manifold, broken vacuum lines, missing bolts, or corrosion on the carburetor (Carter YF). Photograph everything.

Check all fluid levels: pull the oil dipstick (driver's side of block) — if oil is black, sludgy, or smells like fuel that's a red flag. Check coolant in the radiator (NOT the overflow when cold) — look for rust color or oily sheen which indicates head gasket issues.

Inspect the valve cover for oil leaks dried around its edges — very common on the AMC 258. Check the rear of the engine where it meets the bellhousing for rear main seal leakage (dried black oil streaks).

To check if the engine turns: put a 1-1/8" socket on the large crank pulley bolt at the front of the engine (below the radiator). Use a breaker bar — try turning CLOCKWISE only. If it moves even slightly, the engine is not seized. If it's completely stuck, try squirting penetrating oil (Wd-40 or Kroil equivalent) into each spark plug hole and wait 30 minutes before trying again.

Remove all 6 spark plugs (use a 5/8" spark plug socket) and look at their condition: white/chalky = lean or coolant burning, black/sooty = rich or oil burning, normal = tan/light brown. Photograph each one labeled 1-6 front to back.

Check the distributor cap (pull it off — two spring clips) — look for cracks, carbon tracking (black lines inside), and corrosion on the contacts. Check the rotor underneath. These are cheap and should probably be replaced regardless.
Photograph the entire engine bay from multiple angles — top, both sides, from underneath if possible. This becomes your reference for what's missing.
task-sQmcUCW3.guide: This is an assessment task only — no repairs yet. Goal is to understand what the AMC 258 inline-6 actually needs before spending money. The most critical check is whether the engine turns freely, which tells you if it's seized from sitting 8 years.

task-dlWC46Kh.name: Inspect frame rails and body mounts for rust — photograph all problem areas
task-dlWC46Kh.agentRationale: CJ8 frames rust badly at body mount locations and rear crossmember. Extent of rust determines whether original frame is usable or if the spare chassis is needed.

task-pcQuBL7k.name: Inspect body tub floor and firewall for rust
task-pcQuBL7k.agentRationale: CJ8 tubs rust at floor corners and under the carpet/mat. Firewall rust can affect brake and clutch components. Important to document early.

task-MODtq4FJ.name: Check transmission and transfer case condition — look for damage, missing parts, fluid state
task-MODtq4FJ.agentRationale: Trans and transfer have been stored for 8 years. Need to confirm they're complete and in rebuildable/usable condition before planning reinstall.

parts.name: Painless Wiring complete CJ7/CJ8 harness kit (if replacement needed)

task-6sI4TM4o.notes: [3/14/2026] Owner confirms the harness already has many changes and fixes over the years. This is a strong argument against trying to repair further — a heavily patched harness means inherited mistakes, unknown wire gauges, and untraceable faults. Recommendation: during inspection, count the number of old splices/repairs. If more than 5-6, budget for a complete Painless Wiring replacement kit rather than more patches.
task-6sI4TM4o.name: Check wiring harness condition throughout — look for brittle/cracked insulation, rodent damage, missing connectors
task-6sI4TM4o.agentRationale: 35+ year old wiring insulation gets brittle and cracks. 8 years of sitting in a workshop makes rodent damage likely. Electrical issues are a major obstacle to first start.
steps: Start at the fuse block (under dash, driver's side) — open it and photograph every fuse and metal clip. Corrosion here is a red flag for the whole harness.
Trace the engine harness from the fuse block across the engine bay. Gently bend the loom every 20cm — if insulation cracks or flakes off, that section is dead.
Look specifically at every splice, join, or repair you can find. Count them. Write down how many there are. More than 5-6 old splices on one harness = strong argument for full replacement.
Get under the dash with a flashlight and look for mouse damage (chewed wires, droppings, nesting). This is the #1 hidden damage area on stored CJ8s.
Under the car, follow the body/tail harness from the firewall to the rear lights. Note any sections where the loom wrap has disintegrated and wires are loose or exposed.

At the end of the inspection, make a judgment call: if the harness has many old repairs, unknown modifications, or widespread brittle insulation — plan to replace it fully rather than chase electrical gremlins forever. Ask your WhatsApp group if anyone has imported a Painless Wiring kit to Israel — it saves a lot of pain.
task-6sI4TM4o.guide: On a CJ8 with a history of repairs and modifications, wiring inspection is really a decision-making exercise: you're not just looking for damage, you're deciding whether to repair or replace the whole harness. A harness with many previous fixes is a hidden liability — every past splice and wrong-gauge wire is a future fault you didn't create.

task-ZpLtWrMJ.name: Inventory all removed parts and new parts waiting to install
task-ZpLtWrMJ.agentRationale: Car was partially stripped before you bought it and has been sitting 8 years. Without a complete inventory you'll order parts you already have and miss parts you need. Do this before spending a single shekel.

gaps.description: No brake system assessment or rebuild tasks exist in the plan. The car has a new brake booster waiting to install, but the master cylinder, wheel cylinders, brake lines, and drums have no inspection or replacement tasks. On a CJ8 that's been sitting 8+ years, brake fluid absorbs moisture and corrodes internals, rubber hoses crack internally, and wheel cylinders seize. This is safety-critical — the car cannot be driven without a full brake system verification.

Clutch job is planned but no task covers the hydraulic clutch linkage components — specifically the clutch master cylinder and slave cylinder. On a CJ8 that's been sitting 8 years, these are almost certainly seized or leaking. If hydraulic: both should be replaced when doing the clutch. If mechanical linkage: the pivot ball and rod condition need checking. Missing this means the new clutch may go in but the clutch won't function.

No fuel system tasks exist. After 8 years sitting, the fuel tank almost certainly has degraded fuel, varnish deposits, and possible rust or sediment. The Carter YF carburetor will be gummed up. Fuel lines may be cracked or clogged. The car will not start or run reliably without addressing this. Needs at minimum: tank drain/flush/inspection, fuel line inspection, and carb rebuild or replacement.

Battery and charging system not addressed. After 8 years, the battery is certainly dead and the alternator, voltage regulator, and starter motor condition are unknown. Without a confirmed charging system, the engine cannot be started or tested — which blocks all subsequent mechanical verification work.

AMC Model 20 rear axle shaft condition not flagged for inspection. This is a known weak point on CJ8s — the C-clip axle shafts are prone to breaking, especially if the car was used off-road. Before installing the new suspension lift, the rear axle shaft condition (and ideally upgrade to aftermarket shafts) should be assessed. A broken axle shaft on the road means complete loss of drive and potential wheel separation.

decisions.summary: Scaled back from full frame-off restoration to "get it running and roadworthy in good shape"
decisions.rationale: Original full resto plan stalled 8 years ago. Owner wants to actually drive the car rather than pursue a perfect restoration that may never finish.

Owner is hands-on, can do most mechanical work or follow detailed steps. Has full tool set. Part of an Israeli Jeep/mechanics WhatsApp group for community support.
AMC 150 2.5L 4-cylinder (Iron Duke), out of car, sitting separately, unknown condition, hasn't run in years
Car still on original frame. Spare chassis also available.
Clean, reliable, trail-capable daily driver. Smart budget — no wasted money. Not a show car.

Was running/working before disassembly. Taken apart ~8 years ago for a full redo, project stalled halfway through. Partial disassembly — some parts removed, some still on, some new parts purchased and waiting to be installed.
New parts waiting to install: suspension with 2.5" lift kit, clutch, engine mounts, brake booster. Likely more that owner doesn't remember.

Some rust confirmed on both body and frame. Extent unknown — needs inspection.
Owner has a spare chassis available
Basic hand tools, angle grinder, access to a welder
All wiring completely removed from vehicle. Starting from scratch.

researchNotes.finding: The 1989 CJ8 Scrambler is the last year of production, making parts increasingly hard to source. Key chronic issues: (1) AMC Model 20 rear axle — weak C-clip axle shafts prone to breaking under load, especially off-road; (2) AMC 258 inline-6 — rear main seal leaks, worn Carter YF carburetor, head gasket failure on high-mileage units; (3) Frame rust at body mount locations, especially rear crossmembers; (4) Brake master cylinder failure (single-circuit feel), drum brake hardware deterioration; (5) Steering box wear (Ross or Saginaw); (6) Wiring harness brittleness — 35+ year old insulation cracks and shorts; (7) Body tub rust at floor corners and tailgate area; (8) Dana 300 transfer case — generally robust but seals weep; (9) T4/T5 transmission — synchros wear on 2nd gear. The CJ8's longer wheelbase (103.5") makes it rarer and more valuable than CJ7.

researchNotes.topic: CJ8 wiring harness layout and inspection
researchNotes.finding: The CJ8 wiring system is divided into 4 main sections: (1) Engine harness — runs from battery/fuse block through engine bay, connects to alternator, ignition coil, distributor, starter, temperature sender, oil pressure sender; (2) Dash/instrument harness — behind the dashboard, connects gauges, ignition switch, light switches, fuse block; (3) Body/tail harness — runs from firewall along the frame to rear lights, fuel sender, brake lights; (4) Headlight harness — front of car, headlights, turn signals, parking lights. After 8+ years of storage, main failure points are: cracked/brittle insulation (especially near heat sources like exhaust), rodent chewing (especially in enclosed areas like under dash and along frame), corroded connectors (especially ground points on frame/body), and dried-out tape/loom wrapping that falls apart when touched. The fuse block on CJ8 is under the dash on driver's side — fuses and their metal clips corrode badly with age.

For a CJ8 with an already-modified/patched harness, the decision is: (1) Repair existing — only viable if harness is mostly original and damage is isolated. A harness with many previous fixes is a liability — you inherit every past mistake, hidden splice, wrong gauge wire, and unknown modification. (2) Complete replacement — Painless Wiring makes a complete CJ7/CJ8 harness kit (part ~20101 or similar) that includes all 4 harnesses pre-labeled, with modern fuse block. Cost ~$400-600 USD imported to Israel (~₪1,500-2,200). This is actually the cleaner, safer option for a heavily modified old harness. (3) Partial replacement — replace only the harnesses that are damaged. Engine harness and body harness are most commonly available as individual sections. In Israel, the best route is to order from the US (Summit Racing, Quadratec, or Amazon US) and ship via a freight forwarder. Israeli Jeep WhatsApp community likely has experience with this.

researchNotes.topic: AMC 150 2.5L Iron Duke engine — CJ8 specific notes
researchNotes.finding: The 2.5L AMC 150 (also called "Iron Duke" — borrowed from GM) was used in CJ7/CJ8 from 1983-1986, then replaced by the AMC 2.5L inline-4 through 1990. Key characteristics: (1) Robust short block — these engines rarely die from internal wear, but after long storage the main risks are: seized rings from rust inside cylinders, gummed oil passages, cracked or deteriorated valve stem seals, and dried-out gaskets. (2) Common weak points: oil pan gasket, valve cover gasket, front crankshaft seal, carburetor (Motorcraft 2150 or Carter BBD on these), and ignition distributor cap/rotor. (3) After 8 years sitting, the cylinders may have surface rust — a compression test and borescope inspection are essential before attempting to start. (4) The cylinder head on the 2.5L is prone to warping if overheated, but if it was stored drained and dry, warping is unlikely. (5) Good news: if the short block turns freely and compression is within spec (150-180 PSI per cylinder), these engines can often be brought back with a carb rebuild, fresh gaskets, and tune-up. Full rebuild cost in Israel: ~₪8,000-15,000 if needed. Partial refresh (gaskets, seals, carb): ~₪2,000-4,000.

researchNotes.topic: CJ8 bare-frame restoration — correct sequence of operations
researchNotes.finding: When rebuilding a CJ8 from a bare frame up, the correct sequence is: (1) Frame first — treat all rust, repair any structural damage, paint/coat frame BEFORE anything goes on it. Much easier to weld, grind, and coat when bare. (2) Suspension next — install front and rear axles with new suspension while frame is accessible from all sides. Set pinion angles correctly before final torque. (3) Engine and transmission — once frame/suspension is solid, drop in engine, trans, transfer case as an assembly or sequentially. (4) Brake and fuel lines — run hard lines along frame before body goes on. Critical — almost impossible to do properly with body in place. (5) Wiring — run frame/body wiring harness along frame before body. (6) Body tub — lower onto frame last, once everything underneath is done. (7) Engine bay finishing — connect all hoses, wiring, throttle linkage once body is in place. This sequence avoids the #1 mistake: putting the body on too early and making frame/underbody work nearly impossible.

agentHistory.content: worked. took apart 8 years ago to redo. stopped halfway. have some parts off, some on, some new waiting. not sure what works what not. some i remember some not.

That's actually a pretty common situation — a mid-resto stall. The good news is you have a solid base to work from. The challenge is we need to figure out what state everything is actually in before we can plan intelligently.

Let me record what you've told me and then ask the most important question first.Two critical questions before anything else:

1. **Where is the engine?** Still in the car, on a stand, or somewhere else?
2. **What was the original goal of the redo** — full frame-off restoration, mechanical refresh, off-road build, daily driver?

That'll tell me a lot about where we actually stand.

agentHistory.content:
1. still in the car
2. full frame off resto (even have spare chasis), but now I just want it to drive before it dies completely, I mean and be in okay shape. but not as crazy as i intended to in the first place

That's a smart pivot. "Get it running and roadworthy" is a much more achievable goal than full frame-off, and honestly for a '89 CJ8 it's the right call — these things are meant to be driven.Good. Now the most important thing before we build any plan is a **ground truth assessment** — we need to know what's actually on, off, working, or missing.

Two quick questions to shape that:

1. **Is the car on its original frame or the spare chassis?**
2. **Do you have a list anywhere — even rough notes — of what was removed or what new parts you bought?** Or is it mostly "I'll know when I see it"?

agentHistory.content:
1. still on original
2. i have new suspension (inckluding 2.5 inch lift), clutch, engine mounts, break booster, and probably more stuff i forgot
2. removed clutch, gear, transfer, not much more i think. but i have the car in the workshop i can check next week better

That's actually great news — you have most of the hard stuff already bought, and the drivetrain is mostly out which makes access easier. Next week's inspection is the right move before we go too deep into planning.Good. Here's what I suggest: **next week when you're at the workshop, go through a quick checklist** so we can build the plan properly. The key things to document:

**Drivetrain**
- Transmission and transfer case — are they sitting nearby or stored elsewhere?
- Is the clutch that's "off" the old one or the new one?

**Engine**
- Any obvious leaks, damage, missing parts?
- Does it turn over by hand (breaker bar on crank)?

**Body & Frame**
- Visible rust on frame rails, especially under body mounts and rear crossmember
- Floor condition inside the tub

**Misc**
- Any other boxes of parts or new items you can photograph

Even rough photos would help a lot. Don't stress about being thorough — **whatever you find next week, we'll build the plan around it.**

Want me to put together a simple inspection checklist you can take with you to the workshop?

agentHistory.content:
drivetrain stored next to jeep.
no sure about engine.
some rust in body and frame yes.

Good, at least everything's together in one place.

Let me save what we know so far and start roughing out the plan structure. We'll leave gaps where we need the inspection to fill in.Now let me build the initial phase structure around what we know:Good — I've set up the skeleton. Here's where we stand:

**What we have so far:**
- ✅ Drivetrain (trans + transfer) stored nearby, ready to reinstall
- ✅ New: suspension/lift kit, clutch, engine mounts, brake booster
- ⚠️ Engine condition unknown
- ⚠️ Rust extent unknown
- ⚠️ Likely more parts somewhere we haven't accounted for

**The plan right now has 3 phases** — Assessment first, then Engine/Drivetrain, then Chassis. We'll add phases for brakes, electrical, and body once we know more from the inspection.

One important question before next week: **do you have a mechanic helping you, or are you doing this yourself?** That'll affect how I scope the tasks and costs.

Perfect setup honestly — a knowledgeable community plus your own hands-on capability means we can plan most of this as DIY with the option to outsource the tricky bits (alignment, welding, etc.).Now let me start populating Phase 1 with real assessment tasks you can work through next week:Good. Phase 1 is your homework for next week. Here's what to focus on:

**Take to the workshop:**
- Phone with camera (photograph everything rusty)
- Breaker bar + socket for the crank bolt
- A flashlight

**The 3 things that matter most:**
1. 🔑 **Can the engine turn by hand?** — If it's seized, that changes everything
2. 🔑 **How bad is the frame rust?** — Determines if we use original or spare chassis
3. 🔑 **Is the wiring harness intact?** — 8 years sitting can mean mice

Come back after the inspection and we'll turn this into a real action plan with costs. The plan will build itself once we know what we're actually dealing with.

agentHistory.content: where to look? what is most important? do i have plans?

Let me pull up the current plan so you can see exactly where we are.Yes — you have a plan. Phase 1 is your inspection checklist for next week. Here's exactly where to look for each item:

---

**1. Inventory parts** *(garage/workshop sweep)*
- Walk around and pile everything together — boxes, shelves, floor
- New parts: suspension kit, clutch, engine mounts, brake booster
- Photograph everything so you don't forget

**2. Engine** *(open the hood)*
- Look for obvious missing parts, broken bolts, cracked hoses
- Put a breaker bar on the crank pulley bolt (front of engine, big bolt) and try to turn it — if it moves, engine isn't seized

**3. Frame rust** *(get under the car with flashlight)*
- Look along both frame rails front to back
- **Most important spots:** body mount holes, rear crossmember, where outriggers meet the rails — these rot first on CJ8s
- Poke with a screwdriver — if it goes through, it's serious

**4. Body tub floor** *(inside the cabin and bed)*
- Lift any carpet/mats, look at corners and edges of the floor
- Check the firewall (front wall) for rust or holes

**5. Transmission & transfer case** *(wherever they're stored)*
- Are they complete? Any obvious cracks or damage?
- Any fluid dried around them?

**6. Wiring harness**
- Follow the main wire bundle from the engine bay to the firewall
- Look for cracked/melted insulation, chewed wires (mice love these), missing connectors

---

**Priority order if time is short:**
🔴 Engine turns? → 🔴 Frame rust depth → 🟡 Wiring → 🟡 Everything else

Does this help? Take photos of anything that looks bad and share them when you're back — we'll build the real plan from there.

agentHistory.content: i need more details about checking the wiring. plans, explanations, diagrams, not sure- help me. explain this task better

Let me research the specific wiring setup on the '89 CJ8 before I explain — I want to give you accurate info, not generic advice.Good. Based on what I know about the CJ8 specifically, here's a proper breakdown:

---

## 🔌 CJ8 Wiring — How to Inspect It

The wiring is split into **4 separate harnesses**. You check them one by one.

---

### 1. Engine Harness *(engine bay)*
**Where:** Runs from the fuse block (driver's side firewall) across the engine bay
**Connects:** Alternator, ignition coil, distributor, starter, oil pressure sender, temp sender

**What to look for:**
- Insulation that looks **cracked, flaky, or melted** — especially anywhere near the exhaust manifold
- Wires that are **bare copper showing through** the plastic
- Connectors that are **green/white with corrosion** inside
- Any wire that **crumbles when you bend it gently** — that insulation is dead

---

### 2. Dash Harness *(under the dashboard)*
**Where:** Behind the instrument cluster, around the steering column, driver's side under dash
**Connects:** Gauges, ignition switch, headlight switch, fuse block

**What to look for:**
- **Mice damage** — this is the #1 spot they nest. Look for chewed wires, droppings, nesting material
- The **fuse block** (plastic box with metal fuses under dash, driver's side) — open it and look at the metal clips inside. If they're orange/brown with rust, that's a problem
- Wires that have **melted together** from old shorts

---

### 3. Body/Tail Harness *(along the frame, under the car)*
**Where:** Exits the firewall, runs along the driver's side frame rail all the way to the rear lights
**Connects:** Tail lights, brake lights, turn signals, fuel level sender (in the tank)

**What to look for:**
- Follow the wire loom **from the firewall to the back** — it should be held by plastic clips to the frame
- Look for sections where the **loom wrapping has fallen off** and wires are loose
- Check where it passes over/near the **rear axle** — flexing over years cracks insulation there
- Any **missing connectors** at the tail lights

---

### 4. Headlight Harness *(front of car)*
**Where:** From fuse block, runs to both front corners
**Connects:** Headlights, turn signals, parking lights

**What to look for:**
- Connectors at each headlight — do they look intact?
- Any wires that have **rubbed through** on the body

---

## 🔍 Your Inspection Method — Step by Step

1. **Start at the fuse block** (under dash, driver's side) — open it, photograph it
2. **Follow wires into the engine bay** — run your fingers along the loom gently, feel for cracks
3. **Get under the dash with a flashlight** — spend 5 minutes just looking for mouse damage
4. **Get under the car** — follow the wire loom from firewall to the rear
5. **Check each end connector** — tail lights, headlights, any plug you can see

---

## 📋 What to Record

For each harness, note:
- ✅ Looks okay / intact
- ⚠️ Cracked/brittle but wires not exposed
- ❌ Exposed copper, chewed, corroded connectors, or missing sections

---

## Bottom Line
After 8 years sitting, **expect the insulation to be brittle everywhere** — that's normal. The question is whether it's still *protecting* the copper or not. Brittle but intact = manageable. Chewed through or bare copper = needs replacement before you put power to it or you risk a fire.

**Don't connect a battery until you've done this check.**

Does this make sense? Want me to also add a proper wiring inspection task with this breakdown into the plan?

input.topic: CJ8 wiring harness layout and inspection
input.finding: The CJ8 wiring system is divided into 4 main sections: (1) Engine harness — runs from battery/fuse block through engine bay, connects to alternator, ignition coil, distributor, starter, temperature sender, oil pressure sender; (2) Dash/instrument harness — behind the dashboard, connects gauges, ignition switch, light switches, fuse block; (3) Body/tail harness — runs from firewall along the frame to rear lights, fuel sender, brake lights; (4) Headlight harness — front of car, headlights, turn signals, parking lights. After 8+ years of storage, main failure points are: cracked/brittle insulation (especially near heat sources like exhaust), rodent chewing (especially in enclosed areas like under dash and along frame), corroded connectors (especially ground points on frame/body), and dried-out tape/loom wrapping that falls apart when touched. The fuse block on CJ8 is under the dash on driver's side — fuses and their metal clips corrode badly with age.

agentHistory.content: what tools do I need and what parts should I have ready?

Good question — let me save a proper guide into this task right now, including tools and parts.All saved into the task. Here's the summary:

---

## 🔧 Tools You Need

| Tool | What for |
|------|----------|
| **Breaker bar** (long handle) | Turning the crank — you need leverage |
| **1-1/8" socket** | Fits the crank pulley bolt |
| **5/8" spark plug socket** | Removing the 6 spark plugs |
| **Standard ratchet + extensions** | General access |
| **Flathead screwdriver** | Distributor cap clips, prying loom brackets |
| **Flashlight or headlamp** | Looking inside plug holes, dark corners |
| **Phone/camera** | Photograph everything — this is assessment |

---

## 🛒 Parts to Have Ready

These are cheap and you'll need them regardless of what you find:

| Part | Est. Cost |
|------|-----------|
| Penetrating oil (WD-40) | ₪25 |
| Distributor cap (AMC 258) | ₪80 |
| Distributor rotor | ₪35 |
| Spark plugs x6 (Champion RN12YC) | ₪120 |
| **Total** | **~₪260** |

---

**This is assessment only** — don't replace anything yet, just bring these so if you pull the plugs you can put fresh ones back in. The distributor cap and rotor you'll almost certainly replace anyway after 8 years, so might as well have them on hand.

**Most important thing to find out:** does the crank turn? Everything else flows from that answer.

toolCalls.name: set_task_steps

input.guide: This is an assessment task only — no repairs yet. Goal is to understand what the AMC 258 inline-6 actually needs before spending money. The most critical check is whether the engine turns freely, which tells you if it's seized from sitting 8 years.
steps: Remove the air filter housing to get a clear view of the top of the engine — look for cracked intake manifold, broken vacuum lines, missing bolts, or corrosion on the carburetor (Carter YF). Photograph everything.

Check all fluid levels: pull the oil dipstick (driver's side of block) — if oil is black, sludgy, or smells like fuel that's a red flag. Check coolant in the radiator (NOT the overflow when cold) — look for rust color or oily sheen which indicates head gasket issues.

Inspect the valve cover for oil leaks dried around its edges — very common on the AMC 258. Check the rear of the engine where it meets the bellhousing for rear main seal leakage (dried black oil streaks).

To check if the engine turns: put a 1-1/8" socket on the large crank pulley bolt at the front of the engine (below the radiator). Use a breaker bar — try turning CLOCKWISE only. If it moves even slightly, the engine is not seized. If it's completely stuck, try squirting penetrating oil (Wd-40 or Kroil equivalent) into each spark plug hole and wait 30 minutes before trying again.

Remove all 6 spark plugs (use a 5/8" spark plug socket) and look at their condition: white/chalky = lean or coolant burning, black/sooty = rich or oil burning, normal = tan/light brown. Photograph each one labeled 1-6 front to back.

Check the distributor cap (pull it off — two spring clips) — look for cracks, carbon tracking (black lines inside), and corrosion on the contacts. Check the rotor underneath. These are cheap and should probably be replaced regardless.
Photograph the entire engine bay from multiple angles — top, both sides, from underneath if possible. This becomes your reference for what's missing.

agentHistory.content: what to checkin wiring? it is an old harness with many changes and fixes over the years. just look? consider new? hmm

Good question, and the fact that it already has "many changes and fixes" actually matters a lot here. Let me think through this properly and search for what's available in Israel for CJ8 wiring.Let me save what I know from experience on this and update the task properly.All saved into the task. Here's the straight answer:

---

## Should You Repair or Replace?

**The honest answer: a harness with many old fixes is not worth repairing further.**

Every time someone patched it, they introduced:
- Possibly wrong gauge wire
- Splices that corrode over time
- Modifications you don't know about
- Future electrical faults you'll spend days chasing

**So the inspection goal isn't just "is it damaged" — it's "how many old repairs exist."**

---

## The Decision Rule

During inspection, **count the splices and old repairs** you can see:

| What you find | Decision |
|---|---|
| Mostly original, 1-2 old fixes | Repair the damaged sections |
| 5+ old repairs, brittle everywhere | **Replace the whole thing** |
| Mouse damage + many repairs | **Definitely replace** |

---

## If You Replace — What to Buy

**Painless Wiring kit #20101** — covers the full CJ7/CJ8, all 4 harnesses pre-labeled, modern fuse block. About **₪1,500-2,000** imported from the US via a freight forwarder.

**Ask your WhatsApp group** — someone in Israel has almost certainly done this before. That's exactly the kind of question that saves you weeks of research.

---

**Bottom line:** go in with the mindset that you might be replacing it. If it turns out to be clean, great — you saved money. But don't invest hours repairing a harness that's already been hacked multiple times.

toolCalls.name: add_research_note

input.finding: For a CJ8 with an already-modified/patched harness, the decision is: (1) Repair existing — only viable if harness is mostly original and damage is isolated. A harness with many previous fixes is a liability — you inherit every past mistake, hidden splice, wrong gauge wire, and unknown modification. (2) Complete replacement — Painless Wiring makes a complete CJ7/CJ8 harness kit (part ~20101 or similar) that includes all 4 harnesses pre-labeled, with modern fuse block. Cost ~$400-600 USD imported to Israel (~₪1,500-2,200). This is actually the cleaner, safer option for a heavily modified old harness. (3) Partial replacement — replace only the harnesses that are damaged. Engine harness and body harness are most commonly available as individual sections. In Israel, the best route is to order from the US (Summit Racing, Quadratec, or Amazon US) and ship via a freight forwarder. Israeli Jeep WhatsApp community likely has experience with this.

toolCalls.result: Research note saved: "CJ8 wiring harness replacement options"

toolCalls.name: set_task_steps

input.guide: On a CJ8 with a history of repairs and modifications, wiring inspection is really a decision-making exercise: you're not just looking for damage, you're deciding whether to repair or replace the whole harness. A harness with many previous fixes is a hidden liability — every past splice and wrong-gauge wire is a future fault you didn't create.
steps: Start at the fuse block (under dash, driver's side) — open it and photograph every fuse and metal clip. Corrosion here is a red flag for the whole harness.
Trace the engine harness from the fuse block across the engine bay. Gently bend the loom every 20cm — if insulation cracks or flakes off, that section is dead.
Look specifically at every splice, join, or repair you can find. Count them. Write down how many there are. More than 5-6 old splices on one harness = strong argument for full replacement.
Get under the dash with a flashlight and look for mouse damage (chewed wires, droppings, nesting). This is the #1 hidden damage area on stored CJ8s.
Under the car, follow the body/tail harness from the firewall to the rear lights. Note any sections where the loom wrap has disintegrated and wires are loose or exposed.

At the end of the inspection, make a judgment call: if the harness has many old repairs, unknown modifications, or widespread brittle insulation — plan to replace it fully rather than chase electrical gremlins forever. Ask your WhatsApp group if anyone has imported a Painless Wiring kit to Israel — it saves a lot of pain.

Here's what I know: the engine is out and sitting separately (2.5L 4-cylinder, unknown condition — hasn't run in years). The transmission and transfer case are out too. The body tub is off the frame. Frame is bare but I haven't done a full rust assessment yet. All the wiring has been removed. No interior at all. The axles are on the frame I think but not sure of condition. I'm in Israel so parts availability is limited and shipping from the US is expensive. Where do we start and what does the full plan look like?
